# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

**Project ID:** 1
**Document Title:** Exercise Event Monitoring and Display System (EEMDS)
**Prepared By:** Business Analysis Team
**Document Status:** Draft — Pre-Requirements Elicitation
**Applicable Organization:** Department of Homeland Security (DHS) — Exercise Management Division

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The Department of Homeland Security conducts large-scale emergency preparedness exercises that involve coordinating dozens to hundreds of planned Events and Activities across multiple venues and organizational units. These exercises are managed through two existing tools — **Sync Matrix 1.0** and **Exercise Management Tool (EMT) 1.0** — which serve as the authoritative data sources for exercise planning and execution.

During live exercise execution, exercise controllers and observers currently lack a unified, real-time situational awareness display that communicates the status of Events and Activities relative to current exercise time. The absence of such a display creates the following measurable pain points:

- **Delayed awareness of schedule variance:** Controllers cannot quickly determine whether Events are running on time, ahead of schedule, or behind schedule without manually querying source applications, introducing lag in decision-making.
- **Fragmented data access:** Event data exists across two separate applications (Sync Matrix 1.0 and EMT 1.0), requiring users to context-switch between tools during time-sensitive exercise execution.
- **No projection-ready display:** Exercise operations centers frequently use large projection screens for shared situational awareness. No current tool produces a display legible and interpretable at distances of up to 100 feet on a 10×10 projection screen.
- **No temporal filtering or segmentation:** Controllers cannot easily isolate Events by time window, venue, or key event category, forcing manual scanning of large data sets during fast-moving exercise scenarios.
- **No audit trail for changes:** Modifications to Event or Activity data during exercise execution are not tracked, making post-exercise after-action review difficult and incomplete.

The absence of a centralized, real-time, projection-capable display system reduces the effectiveness of exercise execution and limits the organization's ability to conduct meaningful after-action analysis.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The Exercise Event Monitoring and Display System (EEMDS) must achieve the following business objectives, each paired with a measurable success criterion:

| # | Business Objective | Success Criterion / KPI |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Provide real-time situational awareness of exercise Events and Activities | Display data refreshes automatically every 60 seconds; data staleness never exceeds 60 seconds under normal operating conditions |
| 2 | Enable rapid comprehension of schedule variance | Color-coded event status is interpretable by 90% of viewers at 100-foot viewing distance on a 10×10 projection screen |
| 3 | Ensure high availability during exercise operations | System is available 99% of the time during normal business hours over the first six months of operation |
| 4 | Minimize learning curve for exercise controllers | 90% of new users can independently initiate an Event or Activity display within 90 minutes of first use, without formal training |
| 5 | Consolidate data from both source applications | All Events and Activities from Sync Matrix 1.0 and EMT 1.0 are surfaced in a single display without requiring manual data entry |
| 6 | Support post-exercise after-action review | A complete history report of all changes to Event or Activity data is available at the conclusion of each exercise |
| 7 | Restrict access to authorized personnel only | Zero unauthorized access incidents; system correctly distinguishes authorized from unauthorized users in 100% of access attempts |
| 8 | Support large-format projection readability | 90% of viewers can read Event/Activity detail data from 30 feet on a 10×10 projection screen |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Exercise Controller** | Primary end user during live exercise execution | Real-time visibility into Event and Activity status relative to exercise time; ability to filter, pause, and navigate the display |
| **Exercise Director / Senior Observer** | Decision-maker observing exercise from operations center | High-level situational awareness from a distance; ability to determine at a glance whether the exercise is on track |
| **Exercise Planner** | Pre-exercise configuration user | Ability to define time segments, configure display settings, and link Events back to source applications for modification |
| **IT Administrator / System Administrator** | System access and availability manager | User authentication management, system uptime assurance, and configuration of the client environment |
| **Data Entry / Exercise Staff** | Users who modify Event or Activity data during execution | Seamless linkage back to Sync Matrix 1.0 or EMT 1.0 for modifications without leaving the display context |
| **After-Action Review (AAR) Analyst** | Post-exercise auditor | Access to history reports of all changes made to Events or Activities during the exercise for analysis and reporting |
| **DHS IT Compliance / Security Officer** | Regulatory and standards oversight | Confirmation that the system adheres to DHS color schema standards and access control policies |
| **System Integrator** | Technical integration owner | Reliable, stable data interfaces between EEMDS and both Sync Matrix 1.0 and EMT 1.0 |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### Current State

- Exercise Events and Activities are managed in two separate, siloed applications: **Sync Matrix 1.0** (focused on synchronization matrix planning) and **Exercise Management Tool 1.0** (focused on broader exercise management). Neither application provides a unified real-time display suitable for an operations center environment.
- During live exercise execution, controllers must manually check source applications to assess Event status, introducing delays and increasing cognitive load during high-tempo scenarios.
- There is no mechanism to visually communicate schedule variance (e.g., an Event running 15 minutes late) to a room full of observers simultaneously.
- No projection-optimized display exists; ad hoc workarounds (e.g., screen-sharing raw application windows) are not legible at operational distances.
- Changes made to Events or Activities during exercise execution are not systematically logged, making after-action reconstruction of the exercise timeline unreliable.
- There is no ability to filter the exercise event stream by venue or key event category, forcing controllers to manually scan full event lists.
- Local time and exercise time are not displayed simultaneously, creating confusion in exercises where exercise time diverges from wall-clock time.

### Future State

- A single, dedicated **Multi-Document Interface (MDI) display application** aggregates Event and Activity data from both Sync Matrix 1.0 and EMT 1.0 in real time, refreshing every 60 seconds.
- The display is divided into a **graphical region (left two-thirds)** showing Events plotted on a timeline graph, and a **data table region (right one-third)** split between sequential Events (top quarter) and temporally relevant Events (bottom three-quarters).
- Events are **color-coded by variance from current exercise time**, enabling instant visual triage of schedule adherence.
- The display is **projection-optimized**, meeting DHS readability standards at distances up to 100 feet.
- Controllers can **filter Events by venue and key event category**, dramatically reducing cognitive load during execution.
- **Local and exercise time** are displayed simultaneously in separate clocks.
- Users can **pause and resume** the automatic data refresh, supporting briefing moments or display freeze requirements.
- All modifications to Event or Activity data are **logged in a history report**, enabling complete after-action reconstruction.
- **Actual versus assumed time charts** provide quantitative variance analysis for Events and Activities.
- The system enforces **role-based access control**, ensuring only authorized personnel can access the display.

### Gap Summary

| Dimension | Current State | Future State |
|---|---|---|
| Data consolidation | Two separate tools | Single unified display |
| Real-time refresh | Manual, ad hoc | Automated every 60 seconds |
| Schedule variance visibility | Not available | Color-coded, projection-ready |
| Projection readability | Not supported | Legible at 30–100 ft on 10×10 screen |
| Filtering capability | None | By venue and key event |
| Dual-clock display | None | Local and exercise time shown simultaneously |
| Audit trail | None | Full history report of all changes |
| Access control | Undefined | Authorized users only, 100% enforcement |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern system behavior and must be reflected in the detailed requirements:

**BR-01: Data Refresh Cadence**
The display shall automatically refresh all Event and Activity data from source systems on a fixed 60-second cycle. Manual pause of the refresh cycle is permitted, but the system must resume the 60-second cycle upon user command.

**BR-02: Display Layout Proportions**
The display shall always allocate the left two-thirds of the screen to the graphical timeline view and the right one-third to the data table. This ratio is fixed and not user-configurable.

**BR-03: Table Region Segmentation**
Within the data table region, the top one-quarter shall display Events that occur sequentially (order-dependent, not time-bound), and the bottom three-quarters shall display Events ordered by their temporal proximity to current exercise time.

**BR-04: Color Coding for Schedule Variance**
Events and Activities shall be color-coded based on their variance from current exercise time. The specific color thresholds (e.g., on-time, at-risk, overdue) shall conform to DHS color schema standards and must be defined during requirements elaboration.

**BR-05: DHS Color Schema Compliance**
All visual elements of the application — including backgrounds, text, highlights, and status indicators — shall conform to the color schema established by the Department of Homeland Security. No custom or ad hoc color choices are permitted.

**BR-06: Source System Linkage**
Any modification to an Event or Activity must be performed in the originating source application (either Sync Matrix 1.0 or EMT 1.0). EEMDS shall provide a direct link from each displayed Event or Activity back to its record in the appropriate source application. EEMDS itself is a display and navigation tool, not a data editing tool.

**BR-07: Data Scope — MSEL Summary Data**
The data displayed in both the graph nodes and the table rows shall represent Master Scenario Events List (MSEL) Summary data only. Full MSEL detail records are not displayed within EEMDS.

**BR-08: Access Control**
Only users who have been pre-authorized by the system administrator may access the EEMDS application. The system must positively authenticate each user and deny access to any unrecognized or unauthorized user. There are no guest or anonymous access modes.

**BR-09: Time Display**
The system shall display two independent clocks at all times: one showing local (wall-clock) time and one showing current exercise time. These clocks shall be visually distinct and simultaneously visible.

**BR-10: User-Defined Time Segments**
Authorized users may define the time segments used to organize and display Events on the timeline. These segments are configurable per exercise and must be set prior to or during exercise execution.

**BR-11: Filtering Rules**
The system shall support filtering of displayed Events and Activities by at minimum two dimensions: (a) Venue and (b) Key Events designation. Filters may be applied independently or in combination.

**BR-12: History and Audit Logging**
All changes made to Event or Activity data (originating in source systems) that are reflected in EEMDS shall be captured in a history report. This report must be available for retrieval at any point during or after the exercise.

**BR-13: Full Exercise View**
The system shall provide an unfiltered view of all Events in the exercise, accessible on demand, regardless of any active filters.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### In Scope

- A Windows-based MDI client application providing a real-time exercise event monitoring display.
- Integration with **Sync Matrix 1.0** and **Exercise Management Tool 1.0** as read-only data sources.
- Graphical timeline display of Events and Activities.
- Tabular display of Events segmented by sequential and temporal categories.
- Color-coded schedule variance indicators conforming to DHS color schema.
- Dual-clock display (local time and exercise time).
- Filtering by venue and key event category.
- User-configurable time segments.
- Pause and resume of automatic data refresh.
- Direct deep-link navigation from displayed Events back to source applications for modification.
- Actual versus assumed time variance charts for Events and Activities.
- History report of all Event and Activity data changes.
- User authentication and access control.
- Display configuration and modification capabilities.
- Projection-optimized layout meeting DHS readability standards.

### Out of Scope

- Direct editing or creation of Events or Activities within EEMDS (all edits occur in source applications).
- Replacement or modification of Sync Matrix 1.0 or Exercise Management Tool 1.0.
- Mobile or web-based versions of the display (client PC deployment only).
- Integration with any data source other than Sync Matrix 1.0 and EMT 1.0.
- Full MSEL detail record display (only MSEL Summary data is in scope).
- User provisioning or identity management infrastructure (assumed to be managed externally).
- Automated alerting or notification outside the visual display (e.g., email or SMS alerts).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

**Technical Constraints**

- **TC-01:** The system must be deployable on a standard client PC within the exercise operations center environment. No server-side rendering or cloud dependency is assumed for the core display function.
- **TC-02:** The system must integrate with Sync Matrix 1.0 and Exercise Management Tool 1.0 using their existing data interfaces. No modifications to source applications are in scope.
- **TC-03:** The MDI framework must support simultaneous display of both the graphical timeline and the data table within a single application window.
- **TC-04:** The display must render correctly on projection equipment capable of displaying a 10×10 screen image, with font sizes and layout proportions sufficient to meet the readability standards defined in Section 2.

**Regulatory and Standards Constraints**

- **RC-01:** All visual design elements must conform to the **Department of Homeland Security color schema**. Deviation from this schema is not permitted without formal DHS approval.
- **RC-02:** The system must comply with applicable DHS IT security policies governing user authentication and access control for internal operational tools.

**Operational Constraints**

- **OC-01:** The system must achieve **99% availability during normal business hours** over the first six months of operation. Planned maintenance windows must be scheduled outside of normal business hours.
- **OC-02:** The system must support a **60-second data refresh cycle** without perceptible degradation in display performance or usability.
- **OC-03:** The system is intended for use in an operations center environment where projection to a shared screen is a primary use case. All design decisions must account for this deployment context.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

**Assumptions**

- **A-01:** Sync Matrix 1.0 and Exercise Management Tool 1.0 are operational, stable, and accessible from the client PC on which EEMDS is deployed. Their data interfaces (APIs, database connections, or file exports) are available and documented.
- **A-02:** MSEL Summary data is consistently structured and available from both source applications in a format that EEMDS can consume without transformation beyond what is built into the integration layer.
- **A-03:** The exercise operations center has projection equipment capable of displaying a 10×10 screen image connected to the client PC running EEMDS.
- **A-04:** User identity management (i.e., the list of authorized users and their credentials) is maintained externally to EEMDS, either through an existing directory service or a manually managed list administered by the IT Administrator.
- **A-05:** "Normal business hours" for availability purposes is defined as the standard DHS workday (assumed 0800–1800 local time, Monday through Friday), unless otherwise specified by the exercise schedule.
- **A-06:** The DHS color schema referenced in the requirements is a documented, accessible standard that will be provided to the development team prior to UI design.
- **A-07:** Exercise time and local time are distinct values maintained by the source applications or an exercise clock service accessible to EEMDS.
- **A-08:** The definition of "sequential" versus "temporal" Events is governed by a field or attribute already present in the MSEL data from source applications, not a classification to be performed by EEMDS.

**Dependencies**

- **D-01:** Availability and stability of **Sync Matrix 1.0** — EEMDS cannot display Events from this source if the application is unavailable or its data interface changes without notice.
- **D-02:** Availability and stability of **Exercise Management Tool 1.0** — same dependency as D-01.
- **D-03:** Delivery of the **DHS color schema specification** to the development team before UI design begins.
- **D-04:** Completion of **user access provisioning** by the IT Administrator before the system goes live for any exercise.
- **D-05:** Availability of a **suitable client PC and projection infrastructure** in the exercise operations center, configured and tested prior to exercise execution.
- **D-06:** Definition of **time segment structures** by exercise planners prior to exercise execution, as the system requires these to be configured before the display can be meaningfully used.

---

*This document represents the pre-elicitation business context and objectives for the Exercise Event Monitoring and Display System. It is intended to guide requirements elicitation sessions with stakeholders and should be reviewed and validated with the DHS Exercise Management Division before formal requirements are baselined.*

# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Project: Mobile Real Estate Agent Productivity System
**Document Version:** 1.0
**Project ID:** 2
**Classification:** Internal — Business Confidential

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

Real estate agents (realtors) spend a significant portion of their working day in the field — visiting properties, meeting clients, and conducting market research away from the office. Currently, agents are dependent on office-based systems to access Multiple Listing Service (MLS) data, generate Comparative Market Analysis (CMA) reports, manage client contacts, and schedule appointments. This dependency creates a critical productivity gap: when agents are away from the office, they cannot access up-to-date property information, respond quickly to client inquiries, or manage their schedules in real time.

Specific pain points include:

- **Lost sales opportunities:** Agents cannot query MLS listings or generate CMA reports while on-site with a client, forcing them to return to the office or delay responses, which erodes client confidence and risks losing transactions.
- **Scheduling inefficiencies:** Appointment coordination between agents, buyers, and sellers relies on phone calls and manual follow-up, with no automated notification when a party responds to a scheduling request.
- **Data fragmentation:** Contact and appointment data exists only in the office system, meaning agents in the field work from memory or paper notes that quickly become outdated.
- **Navigation overhead:** Agents must use separate tools to obtain driving directions to properties, adding friction to their daily workflow.
- **Market analysis delays:** Producing a CMA report currently requires office access and significant manual effort, slowing down the agent's ability to advise clients on pricing.

The organization currently has approximately 1,000 registered realtors, a number expected to grow fivefold within the next twelve months. The existing office-bound toolset cannot scale to support this growth while also addressing the field productivity gap. A mobile solution is required to empower agents to conduct their full range of professional activities from a smart device, regardless of location or internet connectivity.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The system must achieve the following measurable business objectives:

| Objective | Success Criterion / KPI |
|---|---|
| Enable real-time MLS access in the field | Property search results returned within **30 seconds** of query submission, 100% of the time under normal operating conditions |
| Accelerate CMA report generation | CMA reports generated and delivered within **60 seconds** of criteria entry |
| Maximize agent availability | System available **24 hours per day, 365 days per year**, with a minimum availability rate of **99.9%** (no more than 1 failure per 1,000 access attempts) |
| Keep agent data current | Automatic synchronization with the office system occurring **every hour**, ensuring contacts and appointments are never more than 60 minutes stale |
| Support organizational growth | System capable of supporting **1,000 simultaneous users** at launch, with architecture scalable to **5,000 users** within 12 months |
| Eliminate training overhead | **90% of untrained realtors** able to install the product on their device without printed instructions; system usable by realtors with **no formal training** |
| Protect data integrity | **100% of saved user preferences** restored following any system failure or connectivity interruption |
| Secure system access | **100% of users** authenticated and authorized before accessing any system function; zero unauthorized access incidents |
| Improve appointment responsiveness | Realtors notified automatically when sellers or buyers respond to appointment requests, reducing scheduling lag |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Realtors (Primary End Users)
Realtors are the principal consumers of the system. They operate in the field on smart devices and require seamless access to MLS data, client information, mapping, CMA tools, and scheduling capabilities. Their primary need is a fast, intuitive, self-service tool that requires no training and mirrors the workflows they already follow in their professional practice. They must be able to install the application independently and begin using it immediately.

### 3.2 Real Estate Office / Brokerage (Business Owner)
The brokerage owns the business process and is responsible for maintaining the office-side system with which the mobile application synchronizes. The brokerage's primary need is to ensure that agents in the field remain productive, that MLS listings are kept current, and that client relationships are managed effectively. The brokerage also has a vested interest in system security, ensuring only registered realtors can access proprietary MLS and client data.

### 3.3 Buyers and Sellers (Indirect Stakeholders)
Buyers and sellers interact with the system indirectly through appointment scheduling and communication features. They are the end clients whose experience is improved when agents can respond faster, provide on-the-spot market analysis, and coordinate appointments without delay.

### 3.4 MLS System / Data Provider (External System Integrator)
The MLS is an external data source that the system queries for property listings and to which the system submits new or updated listings. The MLS provider must support the query and update interfaces required by the mobile application. Their primary concern is data integrity and authorized access.

### 3.5 IT / System Administrator
The IT team is responsible for maintaining the office-side synchronization infrastructure, managing user registration and authorization, and ensuring the backend can scale to meet growing user demand. They are not expected to be involved in end-user device installation.

### 3.6 Business Analyst / Project Sponsor
Responsible for defining requirements, validating that the delivered system meets business objectives, and managing stakeholder expectations throughout the project lifecycle.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State
- Realtors access MLS data, generate CMA reports, and manage contacts exclusively through **office-based desktop systems**.
- When in the field, agents rely on **printed property sheets, paper notes, and phone calls** to the office to retrieve information.
- Appointment scheduling is handled manually via phone or email, with no automated notification to the agent when a client responds.
- Driving directions to properties are obtained through separate consumer mapping tools, not integrated with property data.
- There is **no offline capability**; if an agent is in an area with poor connectivity, they have no access to any system data.
- The office system supports the current agent population but has **no defined path to scale** to the anticipated fivefold growth.
- New agents must be **trained on office systems** before they can be productive, creating onboarding overhead.

### 4.2 Future State
- Realtors carry a **smart device application** that provides full access to MLS search, CMA generation, client contacts, appointments, mapping, and communication tools — all from the field.
- The application **synchronizes automatically with the office system every hour**, keeping data current without agent intervention.
- When internet connectivity is unavailable, the application operates in **offline mode**, allowing agents to view previously downloaded search results, CMA reports, and appointments without interruption.
- Appointment scheduling is **automated and notification-driven**: the system calls sellers or buyers to schedule appointments and notifies the realtor immediately when a response is received.
- Driving directions to any selected property are available **within the application**, eliminating the need for separate tools.
- The system scales to support **up to 5,000 registered realtors** simultaneously, accommodating the organization's growth trajectory.
- Installation is **self-service**: a realtor can install the application on their device without printed instructions or IT assistance, enabling rapid onboarding.

### 4.3 The Gap
The gap between current and future state is the absence of a mobile, field-ready, integrated productivity platform. The new system closes this gap by consolidating MLS access, CMA generation, contact management, appointment coordination, mapping, and offline capability into a single smart device application that is secure, scalable, and requires no training to use.

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

**BR-01: Access Control**
Only realtors who are formally registered with the organization may access the system. Every session must begin with authentication and authorization. Unregistered individuals must be denied access at all entry points.

**BR-02: MLS Data Integrity**
The system must prevent the introduction of incorrect or malformed data into MLS listings. All property listing creation and update operations must enforce data validation rules before submission to the MLS.

**BR-03: Synchronization Cadence**
The system shall synchronize contacts and appointment data with the office system on a fixed schedule of once per hour. This synchronization must occur automatically without requiring agent action.

**BR-04: Offline Data Access**
When internet connectivity is unavailable, the system shall permit read-only access to previously downloaded content, including search results, CMA reports, and appointments. Write operations that cannot be completed offline shall be queued for submission upon reconnection.

**BR-05: User Preference Persistence**
User preferences (such as search filters, display settings, and saved results) must be preserved across sessions and system failures. Upon system recovery, 100% of saved preferences must be restored to their last known state.

**BR-06: Appointment Notification**
The system must notify the realtor of new client appointments discovered during automatic synchronization. Additionally, when a seller or buyer responds to an appointment request initiated by the system, the realtor must receive a notification without needing to manually check for updates.

**BR-07: Search Result Retention**
Realtors must be able to save property search results for later review. Saved results must remain accessible in offline mode.

**BR-08: CMA Report Criteria**
CMA reports must be generated based on property criteria specified by the realtor. The system must not generate a CMA report without explicit criteria input from the user.

**BR-09: Map and Directions**
Driving directions and property location maps are available only for properties that exist within the MLS dataset accessible to the authenticated realtor.

**BR-10: Communication Initiation**
The system may initiate calls to sellers or buyers for the purpose of scheduling appointments. Such communications must be traceable to the realtor's account and must only be made to contacts associated with that realtor's client records.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope
- **MLS Query and Search:** Ability for realtors to search MLS property listings by specified criteria, with results displayed including clear property images.
- **MLS Listing Management:** Ability to create new property listings and update existing listings in the MLS from the mobile device.
- **CMA Report Generation:** Ability to generate a Comparative Market Analysis report based on realtor-specified property criteria.
- **Property Mapping and Directions:** Display of a map showing property locations for a specified area, and provision of driving directions to a selected property.
- **Contact and Appointment Management:** Download, view, and management of client contacts and appointments, synchronized with the office system.
- **Appointment Scheduling and Notification:** Ability to call sellers or buyers to schedule appointments; automated notification to the realtor when a client responds to an appointment request; notification of new appointments discovered during synchronization.
- **Offline Mode:** Read access to previously downloaded search results, CMA reports, and appointments when internet connectivity is unavailable.
- **User Preference Management:** Saving and restoration of user preferences across sessions and failure events.
- **Authentication and Authorization:** Secure login and access control for all registered realtors.
- **Self-Service Installation:** Installation process designed for completion by an untrained realtor without printed instructions.
- **Automatic Synchronization:** Hourly synchronization of contacts and appointments with the office system.

### 6.2 Out of Scope
- Back-office MLS administration and database management.
- Financial transaction processing (e.g., offer submission, contract execution, payment processing).
- Document management or e-signature capabilities.
- Integration with third-party CRM systems beyond the defined office system synchronization.
- Web browser-based access (the system is a native smart device application).
- Management of the office-side synchronization server infrastructure (owned by IT).
- Training program development or formal onboarding materials.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints
- **Platform:** The application must run on **Windows CE** and **Palm** operating systems. No other mobile platforms are in scope for this release.
- **UI Standards:** The user interface must conform to the native UI standards and conventions of the target smart device platform, including standard menus and navigation buttons.
- **Performance:** Search results must be returned within 30 seconds; CMA reports within 60 seconds. These are hard performance ceilings, not targets.
- **Scalability:** The system must support 1,000 simultaneous users at launch and be architected to scale to 5,000 users within 12 months without a redesign.
- **Availability:** The system must achieve 99.9% availability (999 out of 1,000 access attempts successful), 24/7/365.
- **Offline Operation:** The application must function in a degraded (read-only) offline mode without any internet connection, using locally cached data.

### 7.2 Operational Constraints
- **No Training Requirement:** The system must be deployable and usable by realtors who have received no formal training on the product. The UI must be self-explanatory and use terminology natural to the real estate profession.
- **Self-Service Installation:** Installation must be completable by an untrained realtor without recourse to separately printed instructions. At least 90% of untrained realtors must succeed in self-installation.
- **Synchronization Window:** The hourly synchronization schedule is a business constraint driven by office system availability and data freshness requirements. More frequent synchronization is not required.

### 7.3 Security Constraints
- Access is restricted exclusively to registered realtors. The system must enforce authentication and authorization on every access attempt.
- The system must include data validation controls to prevent incorrect data from being introduced into MLS records.

### 7.4 Regulatory / Industry Constraints
- Access to MLS data is governed by real estate industry regulations and MLS membership rules. Only authorized, registered realtors may query or modify MLS data.
- Communication with buyers and sellers (e.g., appointment calls) must comply with applicable telecommunications and consumer contact regulations.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions
- **A-01:** All target users are registered realtors who possess a smart device running either Windows CE or Palm OS. The organization is responsible for ensuring device compatibility prior to rollout.
- **A-02:** The office system exposes a stable, documented interface (API or data feed) that the mobile application can use for hourly synchronization of contacts and appointments.
- **A-03:** The MLS provides a programmatic interface (query and update API) that the mobile application can call to retrieve property listings, display images, and submit new or updated listings.
- **A-04:** The organization maintains a registry of registered realtors that the authentication and authorization system can reference. This registry is kept current by the brokerage or IT team.
- **A-05:** Realtors have access to a data connection (cellular or Wi-Fi) sufficient to support synchronization and MLS queries under normal operating conditions. Offline mode is a contingency, not the primary operating mode.
- **A-06:** The mapping and driving directions feature relies on a third-party mapping data provider whose service is available and licensed for use within this application.
- **A-07:** The communication feature (calling sellers/buyers) relies on the smart device's native telephony capability and the availability of contact phone numbers within the client records.
- **A-08:** The current user base of 1,000 realtors is expected to grow to approximately 5,000 within 12 months. This growth projection is provided by business leadership and is treated as a planning assumption for capacity design.
- **A-09:** User preferences and cached data can be stored locally on the smart device with sufficient storage capacity to support offline operation.

### 8.2 Dependencies
- **D-01:** Availability and stability of the office-side synchronization system. If the office system is unavailable, hourly synchronization will fail and agents will operate on stale data until connectivity is restored.
- **D-02:** MLS API availability and uptime. Search, listing creation, and update functionality are entirely dependent on the MLS interface being operational.
- **D-03:** Third-party mapping service availability for driving directions and property location maps.
- **D-04:** Smart device platform SDKs and development tools for Windows CE and Palm OS must be available and supported for the development team.
- **D-05:** The IT team must provision and maintain the backend infrastructure (authentication server, synchronization server, and any middleware) required to support 1,000 to 5,000 concurrent users before the system goes live.
- **D-06:** The realtor registry maintained by the organization must be accurate and current at the time of launch to ensure that authentication and authorization function correctly from day one.

---

*End of Business Requirements Document*

*Prepared by: Business Analysis Team*
*Review Status: Draft — Pending Stakeholder Approval*

# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Nursing Program Scheduling and Management System
### DePaul University — Department of Nursing
**Document Version:** 1.0
**Project ID:** 3
**Prepared by:** Business Analysis Team
**Status:** Draft — Pre-Requirements Elicitation

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The DePaul University Department of Nursing currently manages the scheduling of nursing classes, clinical rotations, and student cohort progression through largely manual processes. Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members are responsible for coordinating a complex web of interdependencies: multiple cohorts of students, each following a defined Program of Study; lecture and clinical class offerings tied to specific quarters; clinical lab sections assigned to external hospital and healthcare sites; and instructor availability across both lecture and clinical settings.

This manual approach introduces significant operational risk. Scheduling conflicts between classes offered to the same cohort go undetected until students or instructors raise complaints. Clinical lab sections are assigned to sites without a centralized record of site capacity, contact information, or scheduling history. Student progress tracking — particularly for part-time students who deviate from the standard sequence — is inconsistent and error-prone. Communication of schedule changes to affected students, instructors, and clinical site administrators relies on ad hoc email or phone contact, meaning that cancellations, section changes, or cohort timeline adjustments are frequently not communicated to all relevant parties in a timely manner.

The volume of errors reported by students related to scheduling and clinical assignments is a measurable and documented pain point. The department has determined that a purpose-built software system is necessary to centralize scheduling data, enforce business rules, automate routine communications, and provide administrators with planning visibility across all cohorts and programs simultaneously.

The absence of a centralized system also creates security and compliance concerns: student personal information and clinical site details are currently accessible to staff members who do not have a legitimate need to view them, with no technical enforcement of access restrictions.

**Measurable Pain Points:**
- Student-reported scheduling errors are at a level the department considers unacceptable; the target is a 50% reduction within the first year of system deployment.
- No single source of truth exists for cohort schedules, clinical site assignments, or student progress, leading to duplicated effort and version-control problems.
- Communication of changes to affected parties is manual, inconsistent, and slow.
- There is no automated conflict detection to prevent two classes for the same cohort from being scheduled at overlapping times.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The system must achieve the following business objectives, each accompanied by measurable success criteria that will be used to evaluate whether the system has delivered its intended value.

### Objective 1: Reduce Scheduling Errors
The system shall enforce scheduling rules and provide guided workflows that prevent administrators from creating conflicting or invalid schedules.

- **KPI:** Student-reported scheduling mistakes shall decrease by at least 50% within the first twelve months of go-live compared to the baseline period immediately prior to deployment.
- **KPI:** At least 2 out of every 3 Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members shall be able to independently complete scheduling and student management tasks using the system without assistance after one day of training.

### Objective 2: Centralize and Automate Scheduling Operations
The system shall serve as the single authoritative source for cohort management, class scheduling, clinical site assignments, and student progress tracking for a minimum of five years.

- **KPI:** The system shall be capable of managing 100% of nursing students and their associated classes for a rolling ten-year horizon.
- **KPI:** All routine scheduling communications (class offerings, schedule changes, clinical section changes) shall be delivered via the system's automated email notification capability, eliminating the need for manual ad hoc outreach.

### Objective 3: Ensure System Availability and Performance During Business Hours
The system shall be reliable and responsive during the hours when Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members conduct their work.

- **KPI:** System uptime shall be at least 95%, measured during the operational window of 8:00 AM to 6:00 PM.
- **KPI:** General student management tasks (adding students, updating records, viewing summaries) shall complete within 5 seconds under normal load.
- **KPI:** Schedule generation operations shall complete within 30 seconds.
- **KPI:** The system shall support a minimum of 10 simultaneous users between 9:00 AM and 5:00 PM without performance degradation.

### Objective 4: Enforce Access Controls and Protect Sensitive Data
The system shall ensure that student personal information and clinical site details are accessible only to specifically authorized individuals.

- **KPI:** Zero unauthorized access incidents to protected data categories (student personal information, clinical site details, CampusConnect interface) following deployment.

### Objective 5: Improve Planning Visibility
The system shall provide administrators with on-demand reporting that supports quarterly planning across all cohorts and programs simultaneously.

- **KPI:** Administrators shall be able to generate a report of needed classes for any given quarter — including sections, labs, and room types — without manual data aggregation.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Program Administrators
**Role:** Primary system operators responsible for managing cohorts, Programs of Study, class scheduling, clinical site assignments, and student records.
**Primary Need:** A centralized, intuitive tool that allows them to build and maintain cohort schedules, track student progress, manage clinical lab sections, and communicate changes to affected parties — all without requiring technical expertise.
**Named Individuals:** Dr. Susan Poslusny, Karen Sysol (also holds elevated access to CampusConnect interface).

### 3.2 Nursing Staff Members
**Role:** Day-to-day operational users who assist Program Administrators in managing student records, cohort assignments, and clinical scheduling.
**Primary Need:** The ability to add and update student records, manage clinical lab section enrollment, and access scheduling information relevant to their responsibilities.

### 3.3 Nursing Students
**Role:** Indirect system users; they are the subjects of the scheduling and progress-tracking functions. They receive automated communications generated by the system.
**Primary Need:** Accurate, timely notification of their class schedules, clinical assignments, and any changes that affect them.

### 3.4 Lecture Instructors
**Role:** Faculty members assigned to teach lecture components of nursing classes. They are stored in the system and receive relevant communications.
**Primary Need:** Timely notification of class assignments, schedule changes, and cancellations.

### 3.5 Clinical Lab Instructors
**Role:** Instructors assigned to supervise students at clinical sites. They are associated with specific clinical lab sections.
**Primary Need:** Accurate assignment information and timely notification of changes to clinical lab sections, including cancellations due to illness or capacity changes.

### 3.6 Clinical Site Administrators
**Role:** Contact persons at external clinical sites (hospitals, healthcare facilities) where nursing students complete their clinical rotations.
**Primary Need:** Accurate scheduling information for labs held at their site and timely notification of changes.

### 3.7 Dr. Julie Donalek
**Role:** Elevated-access administrator with specific authorization to view students' personal information.
**Primary Need:** Secure, role-appropriate access to student personal data for program oversight purposes.

### 3.8 IT / System Integrator
**Role:** Technical team responsible for deploying and maintaining the system within DePaul's existing infrastructure, including integration with CampusConnect, the faculty central server, and the student master server.
**Primary Need:** Clear technical interface specifications and security requirements.

### 3.9 DePaul University Information Security / Compliance
**Role:** Organizational authority responsible for ensuring the system adheres to the university's information security and data privacy policies.
**Primary Need:** Confirmation that the system enforces access controls, uses approved virus protection software, and protects student and clinical data in accordance with institutional policy.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

| Domain | Current State |
|---|---|
| **Cohort & Schedule Management** | Managed through spreadsheets or paper-based records; no automated conflict detection; version control is informal and unreliable. |
| **Clinical Site Management** | Clinical site contact information and lab assignments are maintained in disconnected documents; no centralized record of which labs are scheduled at which sites for a given quarter. |
| **Student Progress Tracking** | Part-time student deviations from the standard sequence are tracked manually; no system enforces or records modified timelines. |
| **Communication** | Changes to schedules, clinical sections, and cohort timelines are communicated via ad hoc email or phone; no guarantee that all affected parties are notified. |
| **Access Control** | No technical enforcement of who may view student personal information or clinical site details; access is governed by informal policy only. |
| **Reporting & Planning** | Generating a cross-cohort view of classes needed for a given quarter requires manual aggregation from multiple sources; time-consuming and error-prone. |
| **System Integration** | No automated interface with CampusConnect, the faculty central server, or the student master server; data must be re-entered manually across systems. |

### 4.2 Future State Capabilities

| Domain | Future State |
|---|---|
| **Cohort & Schedule Management** | A single system maintains all cohort records, Programs of Study, class sequences, and quarter assignments. The system automatically detects and prevents scheduling conflicts for classes within the same cohort. |
| **Clinical Site Management** | All clinical sites, their contact information, and their associated lab sections are maintained in a centralized, access-controlled repository. Administrators can view all labs scheduled at a given site for any quarter. |
| **Student Progress Tracking** | Full-time and part-time student progress is tracked within the system. Staff can modify a part-time student's sequence and timeline, with changes reflected immediately in cohort summaries. |
| **Communication** | The system automatically sends email notifications to all affected parties when schedule changes, clinical section changes, cohort modifications, or student status changes occur. |
| **Access Control** | Role-based access controls are technically enforced. Only designated individuals can access student personal information, clinical site details, and the CampusConnect interface. |
| **Reporting & Planning** | On-demand reports provide cross-cohort planning views for any quarter, including classes needed, sections, labs, and room types. Printable summaries are available for cohorts, clinical sites, clinical labs, and individual students. |
| **System Integration** | The system interfaces with CampusConnect, the faculty central server, and the student master server, reducing manual data re-entry and improving data consistency. |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern how the system must behave. These rules are non-negotiable and must be reflected in system design and functionality.

### 5.1 Cohort and Program Rules
- Every cohort must be associated with exactly one Program of Study offered by the Nursing Department.
- A cohort is uniquely identified by its start month and year.
- Only students who are eligible for a cohort's Program of Study may be added to that cohort.
- Each Program of Study consists of a defined name and a required set of classes, which may include both clinical and non-clinical classes.
- A preferred sequence of classes — including the quarter in which each class will be offered — must be definable for each cohort.

### 5.2 Class and Clinical Classification Rules
- Every class in the system is classified as either a non-clinical class or a clinical class; no class may be both or neither.
- A non-clinical class must specify, at minimum, the course name, lecture room requirements, and instructor needs.
- A clinical class must specify, at minimum, the course name, lecture room requirements, clinical site needs, lecture instructor needs, and clinical lab instructor needs.
- Classes scheduled for the same cohort must not conflict with respect to the day and time they are offered. The system must enforce this rule and prevent conflicting schedules from being saved.

### 5.3 Clinical Lab Section Rules
- A clinical lab section may only be created for an existing clinical class already recorded in the system.
- A clinical lab section must specify the clinical site name, the assigned class instructor, and the day and time of the lab.
- A clinical lab section may only be cancelled if there are no students currently registered for that section. The system must prevent cancellation if any students remain enrolled.
- A student may only be added to a clinical lab section if they are registered for the corresponding clinical class.
- A student may be moved from one clinical lab section to another, provided both sections correspond to the same clinical class.

### 5.4 Student Status Rules
- A nursing student's status is classified as either full-time or part-time.
- Part-time students may have a modified class sequence and timeline that differs from the standard cohort sequence. Staff members must be able to manage this deviation within the system.
- Changes to a student's status (e.g., from full-time to part-time) must trigger automated notification to all affected parties.

### 5.5 Access Control Rules
- Clinical site details (including contact person and contact information) are accessible only to Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members. No other user category may view this information.
- Student personal information is accessible only to Dr. Susan Poslusny and Dr. Julie Donalek. No other user, including other Program Administrators, may access this data.
- The portion of the system that interfaces with CampusConnect — specifically the final class section scheduling function — is accessible only to Dr. Susan Poslusny and Karen Sysol.
- All other system access is restricted to authorized users as defined by the department's information policy.

### 5.6 Communication Rules
- The system shall use stored email addresses as the primary channel for all outbound communications.
- Automated notifications must be sent when any of the following events occur:
  - A student's upcoming class offerings or schedule is published or updated.
  - A clinical section's capacity changes or the section is cancelled.
  - A class is cancelled, its section details change, or its offering for a given quarter changes.
  - A cohort's Program of Study sequence changes or a specific week's schedule is modified (e.g., lab cancelled due to instructor illness).
  - A student's status changes (e.g., full-time to part-time) or a student is moved to a different clinical section.

### 5.7 Data Integrity Rules
- The system must perform basic data integrity validation on all data entry to prevent incorrect or invalid data from being stored.
- All terminology used within the system must be consistent with Standard English and the recognized terminology of the national Nursing Community.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

The following capabilities are within the scope of this system:

- **Program of Study Management:** Creation and modification of Programs of Study, including program name and required class listings.
- **Cohort Management:** Creation, modification, and tracking of student cohorts, including cohort identifier, associated Program of Study, preferred class sequence, and quarterly class assignments.
- **Class Management:** Addition and modification of both clinical and non-clinical classes within the Nursing Department's catalog.
- **Clinical Site Management:** Addition and modification of clinical site records, including site name, contact person, and contact information.
- **Clinical Lab Section Management:** Creation, modification, and cancellation of clinical lab sections; management of student enrollment within sections.
- **Student Record Management:** Addition and modification of nursing student records, including personal information, status, program enrollment, and cohort assignment.
- **Part-Time Student Management:** Ability to modify the class sequence and timeline for individual part-time students.
- **Conflict Detection:** Automated detection and prevention of scheduling conflicts for classes within the same cohort.
- **Reporting:** Generation of printable summaries for cohorts, clinical sites, clinical labs, individual students, and the system log; generation of cross-cohort quarterly planning reports.
- **Automated Email Notifications:** System-generated email communications to affected parties for all defined triggering events.
- **Contact Information Repository:** Storage and maintenance of contact information (email and phone) for all relevant parties including staff, students, lecture instructors, clinical lab instructors, and clinical site administrators.
- **System Integrations:** Interfaces with CampusConnect's central server, the faculty central server, and the student master server.
- **Role-Based Access Control:** Technical enforcement of access restrictions for student personal information, clinical site details, and the CampusConnect interface.
- **System Log:** Maintenance of a system log with a printable summary capability.

### 6.2 Out of Scope

The following are explicitly outside the scope of this system:

- Direct student self-service access to the scheduling system (students receive communications but do not log into or interact with the system directly).
- Financial or billing functions related to tuition, clinical site fees, or instructor compensation.
- Human resources management for faculty or staff (hiring, payroll, performance reviews).
- Management of non-nursing academic programs or departments at DePaul University.
- Replacement or modification of CampusConnect, the faculty central server, or the student master server themselves; this system interfaces with those platforms but does not replace them.
- Mobile application development; the system is browser-based and desktop-based within the defined operating environment.

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints
- The system must operate within the **Windows XP Professional** operating environment as used on computers in the Nursing Department's business offices at DePaul University.
- The system must be accessible via any **HTML-compatible browser**, ensuring compatibility without requiring proprietary browser plugins.
- The system must interface with three existing institutional platforms: **CampusConnect's central server**, the **faculty central server**, and the **student master server**. The system must conform to the interface specifications of these platforms.
- The system must use the **organization's standard virus protection software** for threat monitoring; no alternative security tooling may be substituted.
- The system must support a minimum of **10 simultaneous users** during peak hours (9:00 AM to 5:00 PM) without performance degradation.

### 7.2 Performance Constraints
- General student management task response time: **≤ 5 seconds**.
- Schedule generation response time: **≤ 30 seconds**.
- System availability window: **8:00 AM to 6:00 PM**, with a minimum uptime of **95%** during this window.

### 7.3 Regulatory and Policy Constraints
- The system must protect student personal information in accordance with **DePaul University's information security and data privacy policy**. Given that this involves student educational records, compliance with applicable federal student privacy regulations (such as FERPA) is implied and must be addressed during detailed requirements elicitation.
- Access to student personal information must be restricted to named, authorized individuals only, consistent with the university's data governance policies.
- Clinical site information, which may include protected healthcare facility details, must be restricted to authorized Nursing Department staff only.

### 7.4 Operational Constraints
- The system must be deployable and operable within the **standard business office environment** of the DePaul University Nursing Department, using existing hardware and network infrastructure.
- The system must be learnable by all target users within **one day of training**. This constrains the complexity of the user interface and the depth of workflow design.
- The system must remain viable and capable of managing nursing program operations for a **minimum of five years** from deployment, and must be architected to handle student and class data for a **ten-year horizon**.

### 7.5 Usability Constraints
- All terminology within the system must align with **Standard English** and the vocabulary of the **national Nursing Community**. Domain-specific jargon that is not standard within nursing education must not be introduced.
- The user interface must be sufficiently intuitive that Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members can navigate core workflows without consulting documentation during normal operations.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

- It is assumed that the DePaul University Nursing Department's computers are already running **Windows XP Professional** and that no operating system upgrade is planned within the system's initial deployment period. If hardware or OS upgrades occur, compatibility requirements will need to be re-evaluated.
- It is assumed that all Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members have a working knowledge of nursing program operations, including the concepts of cohorts, Programs of Study, clinical rotations, and quarter-based scheduling. The system does not need to educate users on these concepts, only to support them.
- It is assumed that valid, current email addresses are available for all parties who will receive system-generated notifications (students, instructors, clinical site administrators, staff). The quality of the existing contact data will directly affect the utility of the notification system.
- It is assumed that the department will designate specific named individuals for elevated access roles (student personal information, CampusConnect interface) prior to system deployment, and that these designations will be formally communicated to the development team.
- It is assumed that the Nursing Department's existing Programs of Study, class catalogs, cohort records, and clinical site information will be migrated or entered into the new system as part of the deployment process. Data migration planning is assumed to be a deployment activity, not a system feature.
- It is assumed that the system will be hosted on infrastructure managed by DePaul University's IT department, and that the university's standard virus protection software is already deployed on that infrastructure.
- It is assumed that one day of structured training will be provided to all Program Administrators and Nursing Staff Members prior to go-live, and that training materials will be developed as part of the project.

### 8.2 Dependencies

- **CampusConnect Integration:** The system's ability to interface with CampusConnect's central server is dependent on DePaul University's IT department providing documented API or data exchange specifications for CampusConnect. Delays in obtaining these specifications will affect the development timeline for this integration.
- **Faculty Central Server Integration:** Similarly, integration with the faculty central server requires technical specifications and access credentials from the university's IT infrastructure team.
- **Student Master Server Integration:** Integration with the student master server requires access to student eligibility and enrollment data. The accuracy of the system's student records will depend on the currency and completeness of data in the student master server.
- **Institutional Policy Documentation:** The system's access control and data protection features must be aligned with DePaul University's formal information security policy. This policy document must be provided to the development team before security requirements can be finalized.
- **Named User Designations:** The role-based access control implementation is dependent on the department formally confirming the names and roles of individuals authorized for elevated access (currently identified as Dr. Susan Poslusny, Dr. Julie Donalek, and Karen Sysol). Any changes to these designations after system deployment will require a configuration change process.
- **Clinical Site Data:** The completeness and accuracy of clinical site records (names, contacts, addresses) available at the time of deployment will determine how quickly the system can be operationally useful for clinical scheduling. The department is responsible for providing this data in a usable format prior to go-live.
- **Browser Compatibility:** The system's HTML browser compatibility is dependent on the specific browser versions in use on Nursing Department computers at the time of deployment. The development team must obtain a list of browser versions in use to validate compatibility.

---

*This document represents the business context and requirements baseline as understood prior to formal requirements elicitation. It is intended to guide the requirements engineering process and should be reviewed and validated with all named stakeholders before requirements are finalized. Sections marked with specific named individuals (Dr. Susan Poslusny, Dr. Julie Donalek, Karen Sysol) should be confirmed with the department head before access control specifications are locked.*
