# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Disputes Management System

---

**Document Information**

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| Project ID | 4 |
| Document Title | Business Requirements Document — Disputes Management System |
| Document Status | Draft |
| Intended Audience | Business Stakeholders, Requirements Engineers, Project Sponsors, IT Architecture |

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The organization currently processes credit card and merchant transaction disputes through a fragmented, largely manual workflow that creates significant operational risk, compliance exposure, and customer service deficiencies. Cardmember services representatives and merchant services representatives lack a unified, purpose-built application to initiate, track, and resolve dispute cases — including ticket retrieval requests and chargeback notifications — in a consistent and auditable manner.

The following pain points have been identified as primary drivers for this initiative:

**Operational Inefficiency:** Representatives must navigate multiple disconnected systems to retrieve cardmember account information, transaction statements, and merchant details. This increases average handle time per dispute case and introduces data entry errors when information is re-keyed across systems.

**Compliance and Audit Risk:** The organization is legally obligated to process credit card disputes in accordance with **Regulation E**, **Regulation Z**, and the **Merchant Operating Regulations**. Without a centralized system enforcing these rules programmatically, compliance depends entirely on individual representative knowledge and manual adherence — a posture that is unsustainable and audit-vulnerable. There is currently no reliable, automated audit trail capturing every action taken on a dispute case, which creates significant legal exposure if a dispute is later challenged.

**Access Control Gaps:** There is no systematic enforcement of role-based access to dispute case initiation and follow-up actions. Both internal and external users (e.g., partner institutions accessing the organization's dispute infrastructure) interact with dispute data, yet there is no technical barrier preventing unauthorized users from viewing or modifying cases outside their cardholder base, or from initiating actions they are not authorized to perform.

**Duplicate Dispute Risk:** Without a centralized system, duplicate dispute requests on the same transaction can be submitted, resulting in erroneous financial adjustments to cardmember and merchant accounts and creating reconciliation problems downstream.

**Lack of Real-Time Visibility:** Representatives and supervisors have no real-time view of dispute case status, aging, or pending follow-up actions. Cases that require time-sensitive responses from merchants — particularly chargebacks — may be missed, resulting in financial loss to the organization.

**Data Retention and Database Hygiene:** There is no automated mechanism to purge aged, closed dispute records from the online database, causing performance degradation and storage cost increases over time.

The absence of a purpose-built Disputes application means the organization cannot scale its dispute processing operations, cannot demonstrate consistent regulatory compliance, and cannot provide the level of service quality expected by cardmembers and merchant partners.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The Disputes Management System must achieve the following business objectives, each paired with measurable success criteria:

### Objective 1: Centralize and Standardize Dispute Processing
Provide a single, authoritative application through which all dispute cases — ticket retrieval requests and chargeback notifications — are created, managed, and resolved.

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of dispute case initiations and follow-up actions are performed through the Disputes application; no direct database or data file access is permitted to end users.
- Zero duplicate dispute requests are created on the same transaction.

### Objective 2: Achieve Full Regulatory Compliance
Ensure all dispute processing workflows, business rules, and data handling practices conform to applicable legal and regulatory frameworks.

**Success Criteria:**
- All business rules implemented in the system are demonstrably compliant with Regulation E, Regulation Z, and the Merchant Operating Regulations, as verified by the organization's legal and compliance team prior to go-live.
- Zero regulatory violations attributable to system behavior in the first 12 months of operation.

### Objective 3: Enforce Role-Based Access and Security
Ensure that only authorized users can access the system, and that each user's capabilities are strictly governed by their assigned access level.

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of unauthorized access attempts are blocked by the system.
- External users are technically prevented from accessing dispute cases outside their cardholder base and from requesting original receipts.
- Revoked user accounts cannot be reinstated without System Administrator action.

### Objective 4: Deliver a Complete and Reliable Audit Trail
Maintain a comprehensive, tamper-evident history of all actions taken on every dispute case to support internal auditing and regulatory examination.

**Success Criteria:**
- 100% of user-initiated and system-initiated actions on a dispute case are recorded with the acting user's name (or system identifier), date, and action detail.
- User access changes (additions and modifications) are captured in a User Report available for audit review.

### Objective 5: Meet Performance and Availability Standards
Ensure the system is responsive, reliable, and available to support a 24-hour, multi-user operational environment.

**Success Criteria:**
- Screen-to-screen navigation response time does not exceed **5 seconds** under normal and peak load conditions.
- The system supports a minimum of **350 concurrent users** without measurable performance degradation.
- System availability of **24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year**, with the sole exception of scheduled maintenance windows: Monday through Saturday, 3:00 AM to 4:00 AM EST; Sunday, 1:00 AM to 5:00 AM EST.

### Objective 6: Enable Rapid User Adoption
Ensure the system is intuitive enough that trained representatives can perform their core job functions immediately after a standard training program.

**Success Criteria:**
- **100%** of cardmember and merchant services representatives regularly use the Disputes application following a **2-day training course**.
- **100%** of cardmember services representatives successfully create a dispute case on their first attempt after completing training.
- **100%** of merchant services representatives successfully perform a follow-up action on a dispute case on their first attempt after completing training.

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Need |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardmember Services Representatives** | Primary internal end users responsible for initiating dispute cases on behalf of cardmembers | Ability to quickly search for transactions, initiate ticket retrieval requests and chargebacks, and receive immediate confirmation of case creation |
| **Merchant Services Representatives** | Internal end users responsible for managing ongoing dispute cases and performing follow-up actions | Ability to search for existing dispute cases, view case history, and execute follow-up actions appropriate to case status |
| **External Partner Users** | Users from partner or issuing institutions who access the system to manage disputes within their own cardholder base | View and update access to dispute cases within their authorized cardholder base only; restricted from certain actions available to internal users (e.g., original receipt requests) |
| **System Administrator** | IT or operations staff responsible for user account management and system maintenance | Ability to add users, modify access levels, reinstate revoked accounts, and generate user audit reports |
| **Compliance / Legal Team** | Internal stakeholders responsible for ensuring regulatory adherence | Assurance that all system business rules conform to Regulation E, Regulation Z, and Merchant Operating Regulations |
| **Internal Auditors** | Reviewers of dispute case histories and user access logs | Access to complete, accurate audit trails for dispute cases and user account changes |
| **IT / Systems Integration Team** | Technical team responsible for integrating the Disputes application with upstream and downstream systems | Clear interface specifications for the Statement Database, Cardmember Information Database, Merchant Information Database, Print Letter Utility, and Cardmember Posting and Billing System |
| **Project Sponsor / Business Owner** | Senior business leader accountable for the initiative | On-time, on-budget delivery of a system that reduces compliance risk, improves operational efficiency, and supports business growth |

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### Current State Limitations

| Dimension | Current State |
|---|---|
| **Dispute Initiation** | Representatives initiate disputes through manual processes, potentially involving paper forms, phone calls, or access to multiple disconnected systems. There is no single point of entry. |
| **Transaction Lookup** | Representatives must access separate systems to retrieve statement data, cardmember account details, and merchant information, requiring re-keying of data and increasing error rates. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Compliance with Regulation E, Regulation Z, and Merchant Operating Regulations depends on individual representative knowledge and manual process adherence, with no programmatic enforcement. |
| **Access Control** | There is no systematic, role-based enforcement of who can initiate disputes versus who can only view them. External users may not be adequately restricted from internal-only functions. |
| **Audit Trail** | No automated, centralized record of all actions taken on a dispute case exists. Reconstructing the history of a case for audit or legal purposes is time-consuming and unreliable. |
| **Duplicate Prevention** | No automated check prevents the same transaction from being disputed more than once, creating financial reconciliation risk. |
| **Case Visibility** | Representatives and supervisors have no real-time, color-coded view of case status across the portfolio. Identifying open, aging, or at-risk cases requires manual effort. |
| **Letter and Adjustment Requests** | Requests for letters to merchants and financial adjustments to accounts are handled outside of the dispute workflow, creating coordination gaps and delays. |
| **Data Retention** | Closed dispute records accumulate indefinitely in the database, degrading system performance and increasing storage costs. |
| **Systematic Notifications** | When automated processes (e.g., dispute aging) act on a case, the originating representative is not notified, leading to missed follow-up opportunities. |

### Future State Capabilities

| Dimension | Future State |
|---|---|
| **Dispute Initiation** | A single, unified application provides a guided workflow for initiating ticket retrieval requests and chargeback notifications, with real-time prompts for required information and immediate confirmation upon case creation. |
| **Transaction Lookup** | The system integrates in real time with the Statement Database, Cardmember Information Database, and Merchant Information Database, eliminating manual re-keying and ensuring data accuracy. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | All business rules are programmatically enforced within the application, ensuring 100% compliance with applicable regulations on every transaction processed. |
| **Access Control** | Role-based access (View vs. Update) is enforced at the system level. External users are restricted to their cardholder base and excluded from internal-only functions. Revoked accounts require System Administrator action to reinstate. |
| **Audit Trail** | Every user-initiated and system-initiated action on a dispute case is automatically recorded with user identity, date, and action detail. A User Report captures all account additions and access changes. |
| **Duplicate Prevention** | The system automatically detects and prevents duplicate dispute requests 100% of the time. |
| **Case Visibility** | A color-coded search results interface allows representatives to instantly identify case status (e.g., open cases in yellow, closed cases in gray) across the dispute portfolio. |
| **Letter and Adjustment Requests** | Letter requests are automatically formatted and submitted to the Print Letter Utility; financial adjustment requests are automatically submitted to the Cardmember Posting and Billing System — both as integrated steps within the dispute workflow. |
| **Data Retention** | Dispute cases closed for more than 6 months are automatically purged from the online database, maintaining system performance and storage efficiency. |
| **Systematic Notifications** | When an automated process acts on a dispute case, the originating representative is notified upon their next logon, ensuring timely awareness and follow-up. |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

The following domain-specific rules govern system behavior and must be enforced programmatically at all times:

### Access and Authorization Rules

**BR-01:** Only users with **Update access** are permitted to initiate dispute requests (ticket retrieval requests or chargeback notifications). Users with View-only access may search and view cases but cannot create or modify them.

**BR-02:** The system must authenticate all users prior to granting access. Only users with valid, active accounts in the system may log on.

**BR-03:** Users may only access dispute cases that belong to their authorized cardholder base. Cross-portfolio access is strictly prohibited.

**BR-04:** External users are prohibited from requesting original receipts. This action is restricted to internal users only.

**BR-05:** A revoked user account may only be reinstated by the System Administrator. No other user role has this capability.

**BR-06:** The list of available follow-up actions presented to a user for a given dispute case must be dynamically filtered based on (a) the current status of the case and (b) the access level of the logged-in user.

### Dispute Initiation Rules

**BR-07:** A dispute may only be initiated on transactions that are within the eligible age window as defined by the applicable business rules. The system must filter and present only disputable transactions to the user.

**BR-08:** A single dispute case may be initiated on multiple transactions, provided all transactions belong to a single merchant.

**BR-09:** The system must prevent the creation of duplicate dispute requests. If a dispute already exists for a given transaction, the system must block any subsequent dispute initiation on that same transaction.

**BR-10:** Three types of ticket retrieval requests are supported: (1) Request for Original Receipt, (2) Request for a Copy of the Receipt, and (3) Request for a Portfolio. Each type has distinct eligibility rules (see BR-04).

**BR-11:** Two types of chargeback requests are supported: Pending and Immediate. The system must prompt the user for all required information specific to the chargeback type selected.

**BR-12:** Upon successful creation of a ticket retrieval request, the system must provide a confirmation containing: the dispute case number, the type of retrieval requested, and the date the merchant response is due.

**BR-13:** Upon successful creation of a chargeback request, the system must provide a confirmation containing: the dispute case number, the type of chargeback (pending or immediate), and the date the merchant response is due.

### Reason Code Rules

**BR-14:** The list of available dispute reason codes presented to a user must be dynamically derived based on (a) the type of transaction being disputed and (b) the industry classification of the merchant involved. Generic or unconstrained reason code selection is not permitted.

### Case History and Audit Rules

**BR-15:** Every action that creates or modifies a dispute case — whether initiated by a user or by an automated system process — must be recorded in the case history with the user's name (or system identifier), the date of the action, and a description of the action taken.

**BR-16:** All additions of new users to the system and all modifications to existing users' access levels must be recorded in the User Report, which is available for audit review.

**BR-17:** When a systematic (non-user-initiated) action occurs on a dispute case (e.g., an automated aging process), the user who originally initiated the dispute case must be notified upon their next logon.

### Data Retention Rules

**BR-18:** Dispute cases that have been closed for more than **6 months** must be purged from the online disputes database. This purge process must be systematic and automated.

### Regulatory Compliance Rules

**BR-19:** All business rules implemented in the Disputes System must comply with the guidelines of **Regulation E** and **Regulation Z** governing credit card dispute processing.

**BR-20:** All business rules implemented in the Disputes System must comply with the **Merchant Operating Regulations**.

### Letter and Adjustment Rules

**BR-21:** All letter requests generated by the Disputes System must be formatted in accordance with the specifications required by the Print Letter Utility application before submission.

**BR-22:** All financial adjustment requests (to cardmember or merchant accounts) must be submitted exclusively through the Cardmember Posting and Billing System.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### In Scope

The following capabilities and functions are within the scope of the Disputes Management System:

- **User Authentication and Authorization:** Logon, role-based access control (View and Update levels), account revocation and reinstatement, and User Report generation.
- **Dispute Case Initiation:** Guided creation of ticket retrieval requests (original receipt, copy of receipt, portfolio) and chargeback notifications (pending and immediate) on eligible transactions, including multi-transaction dispute initiation against a single merchant.
- **Dispute Case Search and Viewing:** Search by dispute case number, merchant account number, cardmember account number, and issuer number; filtering by date range, dispute type, case status, and reason code; color-coded results display.
- **Dispute Case Follow-Up Actions:** Status- and role-filtered follow-up actions on existing dispute cases.
- **Case History and Audit Trail:** Automated recording of all user and system actions on dispute cases; notification to originating users of systematic case actions upon next logon.
- **Reason Code Management:** Dynamic, rule-based derivation of eligible reason codes based on transaction type and merchant industry.
- **Duplicate Dispute Prevention:** Automated detection and blocking of duplicate dispute requests.
- **Letter Request Integration:** Formatted letter requests submitted to the Print Letter Utility as part of dispute initiation and follow-up workflows.
- **Financial Adjustment Integration:** Adjustment requests submitted to the Cardmember Posting and Billing System as part of dispute workflows.
- **Data Integration:** Real-time interfaces with the Statement Database, Cardmember Information Database, and Merchant Information Database.
- **Data Retention:** Automated purging of dispute cases closed for more than 6 months from the online database.
- **User Interface:** Compliant, color-coded, real-time user interface accessible by both internal and external users.

### Out of Scope

The following are explicitly outside the scope of this project:

- Development or modification of the Statement Database, Cardmember Information Database, Merchant Information Database, Print Letter Utility, or Cardmember Posting and Billing System. These are existing systems; the Disputes application will interface with them but will not alter them.
- Direct cardmember or merchant self-service dispute submission (the system is designed for internal and partner representative use, not end-consumer access).
- Development of the training curriculum for the 2-day training course (training content is a separate workstream).
- Archival storage or retrieval of dispute cases purged from the online database (purged records are removed from the online system; any archival requirements are addressed separately).
- Modification of the Merchant Operating Regulations, Regulation E, or Regulation Z (the system must comply with these as external constraints).

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### Regulatory and Legal Constraints

**C-01:** The system must comply with **Regulation E** (Electronic Fund Transfer Act) and **Regulation Z** (Truth in Lending Act) as they govern credit card dispute processing. These regulations impose specific timelines, notification requirements, and procedural rules that must be reflected in system business rules.

**C-02:** The system must comply with the **Merchant Operating Regulations**, which govern the rights and obligations of merchants in the dispute process, including chargeback procedures and retrieval request handling.

**C-03:** The system must comply with the organization's **corporate standards for user interface creation**, applicable to both internally and externally used applications. No custom or non-standard UI frameworks may be used without explicit approval.

### Technical Constraints

**C-04:** All cardmember data must be sourced exclusively from the **Cardmember Information Database**. The Disputes application may not maintain its own copy of cardmember master data.

**C-05:** All transaction/statement data must be sourced exclusively from the **Statement Database**. The Disputes application may not maintain its own transaction records.

**C-06:** All merchant data must be sourced exclusively from the **Merchant Information Database**. The Disputes application may not maintain its own merchant master data.

**C-07:** All letter requests must be submitted to and processed by the **Print Letter Utility** application. The Disputes application may not generate or print letters independently.

**C-08:** All financial adjustment requests must be submitted to and processed by the **Cardmember Posting and Billing System**. The Disputes application may not post adjustments directly to any account.

**C-09:** End users must not have direct access to any underlying data files or databases. All data interaction must occur through the Disputes application's user interface.

**C-10:** The system must support a minimum of **350 concurrent users** without performance degradation.

**C-11:** Screen navigation response time must not exceed **5 seconds** under any load condition within the supported concurrency range.

### Operational Constraints

**C-12:** The system must be available **24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year**, with the only permitted downtime being the following scheduled maintenance windows: **Monday through Saturday, 3:00 AM to 4:00 AM EST** and **Sunday, 1:00 AM to 5:00 AM EST**.

**C-13:** The system must be accessible by both **internal users** (employees) and **external users** (partner institution representatives), with appropriate access restrictions applied to each group.

### Training and Adoption Constraints

**C-14:** The system must be sufficiently intuitive and well-designed that **100% adoption** is achievable following a **2-day training course**. This places a significant usability constraint on the design of all user-facing workflows, particularly dispute initiation and follow-up actions.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### Assumptions

**A-01:** The **Statement Database**, **Cardmember Information Database**, and **Merchant Information Database** are operational, stable, and capable of supporting real-time query volumes generated by up to 350 concurrent Disputes application users without requiring modification.

**A-02:** The **Print Letter Utility** application is operational and has a defined, documented interface specification that the Disputes application can implement for letter request submission.

**A-03:** The **Cardmember Posting and Billing System** is operational and has a defined, documented interface specification that the Disputes application can implement for financial adjustment request submission.

**A-04:** The organization's legal and compliance team will provide a complete, current interpretation of Regulation E, Regulation Z, and the Merchant Operating Regulations to the project team in time to inform system design and business rule implementation.

**A-05:** Corporate UI standards documentation is available and current, and will be provided to the development team at the outset of the project.

**A-06:** A user provisioning process exists (or will be established) by which the System Administrator can add users, assign access levels, and manage account status within the Disputes application.

**A-07:** The organization's network and hosting infrastructure is capable of supporting the availability, performance, and concurrency requirements specified in this document (24x7x365 availability, 350 concurrent users, sub-5-second response times).

**A-08:** External partner users will access the Disputes application over a secure network connection (e.g., VPN or dedicated circuit). Network security for external access is managed outside the scope of this project.

**A-09:** The 2-day training course will be developed and delivered by a separate workstream prior to system go-live. The Disputes application project team is responsible for ensuring the system is usable within that training constraint, but not for developing the training materials themselves.

**A-10:** The definition of "disputable transaction age" (i.e., the maximum age of a transaction that is eligible for dispute) is governed by the applicable regulations and Merchant Operating Regulations and will be provided to the project team as a specific, implementable business rule.

### Dependencies

**D-01:** The Disputes application's dispute initiation and follow-up workflows are dependent on real-time data availability from the Statement Database, Cardmember Information Database, and Merchant Information Database. Any outage or performance degradation in these systems will directly impact Disputes application functionality.

**D-02:** The letter generation workflow within the Disputes application is dependent on the availability and correct operation of the Print Letter Utility. Disputes application go-live should be coordinated with confirmation of Print Letter Utility readiness.

**D-03:** The financial adjustment workflow within the Disputes application is dependent on the availability and correct operation of the Cardmember Posting and Billing System. Disputes application go-live should be coordinated with confirmation of Cardmember Posting and Billing System readiness.

**D-04:** The User Report and audit trail capabilities are dependent on the organization having defined audit review processes and designated auditors who will consume these outputs. The value of these features is contingent on their active use by the audit function.

**D-05:** Regulatory compliance of the system's business rules is dependent on the legal and compliance team's timely review and sign-off prior to user acceptance testing and go-live.

---

*End of Business Requirements Document*

*This document represents the business-level requirements for the Disputes Management System and is intended to serve as the foundation for formal requirements elicitation, system design, and project planning activities. All sections are subject to review and approval by the designated project sponsor and key stakeholders prior to the commencement of detailed requirements specification.*

# Business Requirements Document (BRD)

## Collision Estimatics & Recycled Parts Management System

---

**Document Version:** 1.0
**Status:** Draft — Pre-Requirements Elicitation
**Prepared By:** Business Analysis Team
**Business Sponsor:** Claims Operations & Collision Estimatics Division

---

## 1. PROBLEM STATEMENT

The insurance claims and collision repair industry faces mounting pressure to reduce claim costs, improve cycle time, and demonstrate regulatory compliance around the use of recycled (salvage) parts in collision estimates. Today, collision estimators and insurance adjusters operate in a fragmented environment where:

- **Recycled parts sourcing is manual and inconsistent.** Estimators have no standardized, integrated tool to search for available recycled parts from preferred suppliers. This results in underutilization of lower-cost recycled parts, inflating claim costs and reducing competitive margins.
- **Preferred repair facility selection lacks data-driven guidance.** Adjusters currently assign repair facilities based on informal knowledge or static lists rather than dynamic, ratings-based criteria tied to actual recycled parts usage performance. This leads to inconsistent repair quality and missed cost-saving opportunities.
- **Audit capability for recycled parts compliance is absent or manual.** Adjusters who supervise estimates have no automated mechanism to audit whether estimators appropriately applied available recycled parts to a given collision estimate. Without this, the organization cannot demonstrate compliance with estimatics laws governing recycled parts usage, nor can it hold repair facilities accountable.
- **Repair facility performance ratings are not systematically captured or shared.** Ratings based on recycled parts usage behavior are either not recorded or exist in siloed, non-shareable formats, preventing supervisory adjusters from making informed decisions over time.
- **Multi-company data isolation is not enforced.** In a shared-services or multi-carrier environment, there is risk that one insurance company's claim data could be visible to another, creating legal and regulatory exposure.

The cumulative effect is higher-than-necessary claim costs, inconsistent compliance with recycled parts estimatics regulations, and an inability to measure or improve repair facility performance in a structured way. This project addresses these gaps by delivering an integrated web-based system that supports the full workflow from recycled parts search through estimate review, audit, and repair facility rating.

---

## 2. BUSINESS OBJECTIVES & SUCCESS CRITERIA

The system must achieve the following measurable business objectives within defined timeframes:

### 2.1 Business Objectives

| # | Objective | Rationale |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| BO-1 | Increase the utilization of recycled parts in collision estimates | Reduces average claim cost; supports regulatory compliance |
| BO-2 | Standardize and automate preferred repair facility selection | Improves consistency of repair quality and cost outcomes |
| BO-3 | Enable supervisory adjusters to audit recycled parts usage on estimates | Supports compliance with estimatics laws and internal governance |
| BO-4 | Capture and share repair facility performance ratings based on recycled parts behavior | Creates a feedback loop for continuous improvement of repair network |
| BO-5 | Improve productivity of collision estimators | Reduces time-per-estimate and manual effort in parts sourcing |

### 2.2 Success Criteria & KPIs

| KPI | Target | Measurement Method |
|-----|--------|--------------------|
| Estimator productivity improvement | 80% of collision estimators agree productivity has increased | User survey at 1-month post-launch |
| User ease-of-use | 95% of adjusters and collision estimators find the product easy to use | Post-training and post-go-live survey |
| Overall user satisfaction | 85% of all users satisfied with the product | Periodic user satisfaction survey |
| Learnability | Full proficiency achieved within two days of onsite training | Training completion assessment |
| Recycled parts search response time | Search results returned in under 15 seconds | System performance monitoring |
| Preferred repair facility search response time | Results returned in under 8 seconds | System performance monitoring |
| Audit report generation time | Report returned to user within 10 seconds | System performance monitoring |
| Ratings save time | Save operation completes within 5 seconds | System performance monitoring |
| System uptime | 98% uptime during scheduled availability windows | Infrastructure monitoring logs |
| Concurrent user capacity at launch | Support up to 1,500 simultaneous users | Load testing |
| Concurrent user capacity at Year 2 | Support up to 10,000 concurrent users | Capacity planning and load testing |
| Daily recycled parts data volume | Handle approximately 50,000 recycled parts records per day | Data throughput monitoring |
| Help accessibility | In-system help available in 90% of application screens | UX audit |

---

## 3. STAKEHOLDERS

### 3.1 Primary Users

**Collision Estimator**
The collision estimator works at or visits repair facilities to assess vehicle damage and produce collision estimates. They operate in physically demanding environments — repair shops that are dirty, noisy, and not office-like. Their primary need is a fast, simple tool to search for available recycled parts from preferred suppliers and apply those parts to an estimate. They are not responsible for audit or facility rating functions. Productivity improvement is a key success measure for this group.

**Adjuster**
The adjuster reviews completed collision estimates and is responsible for claim accuracy and compliance. They need the ability to assign a preferred repair facility to an estimate, override that assignment when necessary, and request a recycled parts audit of an estimate. Standard adjusters can view audit reports but cannot update repair facility ratings.

**Supervisor Adjuster**
A senior adjuster with supervisory authority. In addition to standard adjuster capabilities, this role is responsible for reviewing audit results and updating preferred repair facility ratings based on observed recycled parts usage behavior. This role has elevated system privileges and is the only role authorized to modify ratings.

### 3.2 Internal Stakeholders

**Claims Operations Management**
Responsible for overall claim cost management and compliance. They are the primary business sponsor and will measure success through claim cost trends and audit compliance rates.

**Corporate IT / Middleware Technology Team**
Responsible for product installations, upgrades, and integration with enterprise systems. They manage deployment within established launch timeframes and maintain the integration with the ChoiceParts recycled parts data feed.

**Corporate Support Center**
Provides end-user support for the product. They need the system to be supportable within existing corporate support processes and tooling.

**Corporate Architecture & UI Standards Team**
Ensures the product adheres to enterprise architecture guidelines, UI standards, and corporate color schemes. They are a governance stakeholder rather than an end user.

### 3.3 External / Regulatory Stakeholders

**ChoiceParts System (External Data Provider)**
Provides the feed of recycled parts data that the system depends upon. This is a third-party system integration, not a human stakeholder, but it represents a critical external dependency.

**Regulatory Bodies**
The system must comply with estimatics laws governing recycled parts usage and insurance regulations governing claims processing, as well as Sarbanes-Oxley financial controls. These bodies are not direct users but their requirements shape system behavior.

**Insurance Company Clients**
In a multi-carrier deployment model, individual insurance companies are clients whose claim data must be strictly isolated from one another.

---

## 4. CURRENT STATE vs. FUTURE STATE

### 4.1 Current State Limitations

| Area | Current State |
|------|--------------|
| Recycled parts search | Manual process; estimators contact suppliers individually or use disconnected tools. No integration with a centralized recycled parts data source. |
| Preferred repair facility selection | Adjusters rely on informal knowledge, static lists, or personal relationships. No ratings-based, data-driven selection mechanism exists. |
| Recycled parts audit | No automated audit capability. Supervisors cannot easily determine whether available recycled parts were applied to an estimate, making compliance verification labor-intensive or impossible at scale. |
| Repair facility ratings | Ratings, if they exist, are informal and not shared across adjusters or persisted in a system of record. |
| Data security | No enforced data isolation between insurance company clients in shared environments. |
| Compliance reporting | No structured mechanism to demonstrate compliance with estimatics laws on recycled parts usage. |
| User experience | No standardized tool; estimators and adjusters use a mix of manual processes, spreadsheets, and disconnected applications. |

### 4.2 Future State Capabilities

| Area | Future State |
|------|-------------|
| Recycled parts search | Estimators search for available recycled parts by vehicle and damaged parts information, filtered by preferred suppliers, with automatic fallback to a 30-mile radius ZIP code search. Results are sourced from the ChoiceParts integration updated nightly. |
| Preferred repair facility selection | System automatically identifies the highest-rated repair facility matching vehicle location and radius criteria. Adjusters can accept or override the recommendation. |
| Recycled parts audit | Adjusters can request an automated audit report that compares available recycled parts from the original search against what was actually applied to the estimate, producing a scored report with percentage utilization metrics. |
| Repair facility ratings | Supervisor adjusters can view, enter, and save ratings (on a 1–10 scale) for repair facilities across defined categories, creating a persistent, shared ratings record that improves over time. |
| Data security | Strict role-based access control and company-level data isolation enforced by the system. |
| Compliance | System behavior and audit trail support compliance with estimatics laws, insurance regulations, and Sarbanes-Oxley requirements. |
| User experience | A single, web-based, professionally designed application accessible from repair facility environments, with in-system help and a two-day learning curve. |

---

## 5. BUSINESS RULES

### 5.1 Access Control & Role-Based Permissions

- **BR-01:** Only users with the Collision Estimator role may search for recycled parts. No other role shall have access to this function.
- **BR-02:** Only users with the Adjuster role may request a recycled parts audit report. Non-adjuster users shall not have access to this function.
- **BR-03:** Only users with the Supervisor Adjuster role may update (create or modify) preferred repair facility ratings. Standard adjusters and estimators may view ratings but not modify them.
- **BR-04:** One insurance company's claim data shall never be visible to users of another insurance company, regardless of shared infrastructure.

### 5.2 Recycled Parts Search Rules

- **BR-05:** The primary recycled parts search shall be conducted against the estimator's list of preferred parts suppliers.
- **BR-06:** If no recycled parts are found from the preferred suppliers, the system shall automatically expand the search to a 30-mile radius from the vehicle's ZIP code.
- **BR-07:** An estimator shall not apply recycled parts to a collision estimate if the search returns no available parts.
- **BR-08:** The system shall retain the recycled parts search results associated with an estimate for later retrieval during the audit process.

### 5.3 Preferred Repair Facility Search Rules

- **BR-09:** The preferred repair facility search shall use vehicle information (year, make, model), vehicle location (street address, city, state, ZIP code), and a search radius between 1 and 30 miles.
- **BR-10:** If the ZIP code is unknown, the system shall resolve it from the street address, city, and state provided.
- **BR-11:** The system shall return the repair facility with the highest ratings that matches the search criteria as the preferred facility.
- **BR-12:** If a preferred repair facility cannot be determined (e.g., no rated facilities exist within the radius), the system shall return a list of repair facilities within the specified radius for the adjuster to select from.
- **BR-13:** The adjuster may override the system-recommended preferred repair facility on the estimate assignment.

### 5.4 Audit Report Rules

- **BR-14:** The audit report shall be generated based on the recycled parts search results originally returned to the estimator and the parts actually applied to the collision estimate.
- **BR-15:** The audit report shall include: total number of recycled parts used in the estimate; total number of available recycled parts from the original search; percentage of available recycled parts that were used; and a total audit score that sums individual line item scores.
- **BR-16:** Audit reports shall be accessible to other adjusters at later points in time, not only the requesting adjuster.

### 5.5 Repair Facility Ratings Rules

- **BR-17:** Ratings shall be entered on a scale of 1 to 10.
- **BR-18:** Rating categories shall include, at minimum, attempted use of recycled parts and actual use of recycled parts.
- **BR-19:** If no ratings have been previously defined for a facility, the system shall display a blank ratings form rather than an error.
- **BR-20:** Ratings shall be entered by the supervisor adjuster based on the findings of the audit report.

### 5.6 Data Integrity Rules

- **BR-21:** The system shall validate all data entered to prevent invalid data from being persisted.
- **BR-22:** The ChoiceParts data feed shall be synchronized once per day at approximately 1:00 AM.

---

## 6. SCOPE

### 6.1 In Scope

- Web-based application accessible via Internet Explorer 5.5+ and Mozilla Firefox 1.0+
- Recycled parts search functionality for collision estimators, integrated with the ChoiceParts system
- Preferred repair facility search and assignment functionality for adjusters
- Recycled parts audit report generation and display for adjusters
- Preferred repair facility ratings management (view and update) for supervisor adjusters
- Role-based access control enforcing the three user roles: Collision Estimator, Adjuster, Supervisor Adjuster
- Company-level data isolation in a multi-carrier environment
- Nightly integration with the ChoiceParts system (approximately 1:00 AM daily)
- ZIP code resolution from address data
- In-system user help covering 90% of application screens
- Compliance with estimatics laws, insurance regulations, and Sarbanes-Oxley

### 6.2 Out of Scope

- Direct integration with collision estimating software platforms (e.g., Mitchell, CCC, Audatex) — the system produces outputs that adjusters and estimators use within their workflow, but direct API integration with third-party estimating platforms is not included in this release
- Mobile native application development — the system is a web application; mobile-optimized native apps are not in scope
- Management of the ChoiceParts data feed itself — the organization is a consumer of this feed, not its operator
- Repair facility onboarding or management (adding/removing facilities from the network) — this is assumed to be managed through a separate administrative process
- Financial transaction processing or payment of claims
- Historical data migration from prior manual processes or spreadsheets
- Self-service user account management — user provisioning and role assignment is assumed to be handled through existing corporate identity management processes

---

## 7. CONSTRAINTS

### 7.1 Technical Constraints

- **TC-01:** The product must run on existing corporate hardware infrastructure across all environments (development, test, production). No new hardware procurement is budgeted for this project.
- **TC-02:** The product must support Internet Explorer 5.5 and above and Mozilla Firefox 1.0 and above. No other browsers are required.
- **TC-03:** The product must adhere to corporate Architecture guidelines, which govern technology stack selection, integration patterns, and deployment architecture.
- **TC-04:** The product must comply with corporate User Interface Guidelines and corporate color scheme standards.
- **TC-05:** Product installations and upgrades shall be managed exclusively by the middleware technology team within established corporate launch timeframes. The project team does not have independent deployment authority.
- **TC-06:** The system must be operable in physically demanding repair facility environments — dirty, noisy conditions with limited controlled workspace — which constrains the hardware form factor and interface design for estimator-facing functions.
- **TC-07:** The system must be capable of handling 1,500 simultaneous users at launch and must be architected to scale to 10,000 concurrent users within two years without a fundamental redesign.

### 7.2 Operational Constraints

- **TC-08:** System availability must conform to the corporate online availability schedule. Unplanned downtime must not exceed 2% of scheduled available time (98% uptime SLA).
- **TC-09:** All maintenance activities must fall within established corporate maintenance windows.
- **TC-10:** End-user support must be delivered through the existing corporate support center. No dedicated support team will be created for this product.
- **TC-11:** The ChoiceParts integration window is fixed at approximately 1:00 AM daily. The system design must accommodate this constraint, meaning recycled parts data will be at most 24 hours old during business hours.

### 7.3 Regulatory & Compliance Constraints

- **TC-12:** The product must comply with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX), particularly as it relates to financial controls and auditability of claims-related data.
- **TC-13:** The product must comply with estimatics laws governing the use of recycled parts in collision repair estimates. This includes ensuring that the audit trail supports regulatory examination.
- **TC-14:** The product must comply with applicable insurance regulations governing claims processing in the jurisdictions where it is deployed.
- **TC-15:** The product must be free of computer viruses and must include protections against malicious attacks, including denial-of-service attacks.

---

## 8. ASSUMPTIONS & DEPENDENCIES

### 8.1 Assumptions

- **A-01:** The three user roles — Collision Estimator, Adjuster, and Supervisor Adjuster — are well-defined within the organization's existing identity and access management infrastructure, and users can be assigned to these roles prior to system launch.
- **A-02:** The organization has an existing corporate support center with the capacity and processes to absorb support for this new product without significant additional investment.
- **A-03:** Onsite training delivery (two days per cohort) is feasible and will be funded and scheduled by the business sponsor prior to go-live.
- **A-04:** The existing hardware infrastructure is sufficient to support the initial 1,500 concurrent user load. Capacity planning for the Year 2 target of 10,000 concurrent users will be addressed in a subsequent infrastructure review.
- **A-05:** The ChoiceParts system will reliably provide its data feed at approximately 1:00 AM daily. Any failures in the ChoiceParts feed are the responsibility of the ChoiceParts provider and are outside the scope of this system's SLA.
- **A-06:** Repair facility data (names, addresses, network membership) is available and maintained in a source system that this product can reference. The project does not need to build a repair facility master data management capability from scratch.
- **A-07:** ZIP code resolution (deriving ZIP from address, city, and state) can be accomplished using an available geocoding or address validation service accessible within the corporate environment.
- **A-08:** The corporate Architecture guidelines, UI guidelines, and color scheme standards are documented and available to the development team at the start of the project.
- **A-09:** Users will access the system via corporate-managed or repair-facility-provided devices running supported browser versions. The project team is not responsible for end-user device procurement or browser upgrades.
- **A-10:** The volume of 50,000 recycled parts records per day represents the expected steady-state load. Seasonal or event-driven spikes have not been quantified and are assumed to be within a manageable variance of this figure.

### 8.2 Dependencies

| Dependency | Owner | Risk if Unavailable |
|------------|-------|---------------------|
| ChoiceParts system data feed | ChoiceParts (external vendor) | Recycled parts search functionality is entirely unavailable; core estimator workflow is blocked |
| Corporate identity and access management system | Corporate IT | Role-based access control cannot be enforced; system cannot be launched |
| Corporate hardware infrastructure | Corporate IT / Infrastructure | System cannot be deployed; all environments are blocked |
| ZIP code / address resolution service | Corporate IT or third-party | Preferred repair facility search degrades for users who do not know their ZIP code |
| Corporate Architecture and UI standards documentation | Corporate Architecture Team | Development cannot begin; risk of rework if standards are applied retroactively |
| Middleware technology team availability | Corporate IT | Deployments and upgrades cannot proceed; go-live date is at risk |
| Repair facility master data | Claims Operations / Network Management | Preferred repair facility search returns no results; ratings cannot be associated with facilities |
| Estimatics law and insurance regulation guidance | Legal / Compliance | System design may not meet compliance requirements; regulatory risk at launch |
